answer: Jamsay is an SOV language. Adjectives, possessives ('kan'), and determiners ('sabu' for all/the plural) precede nouns. Locations use postpositions (e.g., 'ɔju' on, 'wro' in, 'gnin' around?). Cause clauses introduced by 'fu' (or 'systm' variants). Aspect 'have already' marked by pre-verbal particles varying by person/subjectivity (e.g., 'la' 2sg, 'kus' 3sg, 'ku' 1sg, 'gninku' 3sg). Pronouns vary by clause type (main 1sg 'm'/'dʒu', sub 'ju'; 2sg 'wò'/'anu'). Tag questions append 'la:' or 'a:' with dying-quail intonation. Objects coordinate by juxtaposition. Tonal (high ´, low `, rising ^, falling v) with length (:) and nasalization (~). No plural marker evident; context/inflection.
reasoning: Patterns from 12 examples: Consistent SOV (e.g., 7 S cat V ro O old house; 12 S I O mangoes V have-eaten). Possessives before noun (5 'kan' his new house). All before noun (3,9 'sabu'). Because after main O (3,6,9 'fu' introduces sub SOV). Aspect prefixes vary (3 'kus', 5 'la', 12 'beku', 10 'gninku'). Pronoun shifts in embedded (6 'dʒu' main, 'ju' sub; 9 'anu' main). Tags at end (5,10 'a:/la:'). Coordination juxtaposed (9 and). Derived solely from data; no external access. Diacritics from notes integrated into observations.
